draft.introduction: Ephesians 6:10–20 presents the “armor of God” passage, framing the spiritual life as combat against cosmic powers. The military metaphor draws on Isaiah 59:17 and Wisdom of Solomon 5:17–20. From Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: Finally, be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might. Put on the whole armor of God, that you may be able to stand against the schemes of the devil. For we do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers over this present darkness, against the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly places.
translation.original_text: του λοιπου ενδυναμουσθε εν κυριω και εν τω κρατει της ισχυος αυτου

translation.text: Stand therefore, having fastened on the belt of truth, and having put on the breastplate of righteousness, and having shod your feet with the readiness given by the gospel of peace. In all circumstances take up the shield of faith, with which you can extinguish all the flaming darts of the evil one; and take the helmet of salvation, and the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God.
translation.original_text: στητε ουν περιζωσαμενοι την οσφυν υμων εν αληθεια

translator_notes.note: The “cosmic powers” (κοσμοκρατορες) are a distinct category from demons or fallen angels. The term appears in astrological and Gnostic literature to denote planetary rulers who control human destiny. Paul appropriates the language to assert Christ’s authority over all such powers.
